title: AI Coding Workflows
lesson:
An AI coding workflow is the day-to-day way a developer uses AI to write, change, and ship software. It is not a single magic button. It is a habit: how you set things up, what you ask for, how you check the result, and how you keep the AI from quietly making a mess. Get the workflow right and the AI feels like a fast, tireless teammate. Get it wrong and you spend more time cleaning up than you saved.

The three tools you will meet

Most AI coding today happens through one of three setups, and they sit on a spectrum from "helper" to "worker":

  • Autocomplete (like GitHub Copilot's inline suggestions): it finishes the line or block you are typing. You stay in the driver's seat.
  • Chat in the editor (Cursor, the Copilot chat panel): you select code, ask a question or request a change, and the AI proposes an edit you approve. This is a copilot — it suggests, you decide.
  • Agents (Claude Code, Cursor's agent mode, OpenAI's Codex): you give a goal in plain English and the AI runs on its own — reading files, writing code, running tests, fixing what broke — looping until it thinks it is done. This is an AI agent following an agentic workflow.

Analogy 1: a kitchen, not a vending machine

A vending machine gives you exactly one thing for one button. An AI coding tool is more like an eager line cook in your kitchen. If you say "make food," you get something edible but random. If you say "make a margherita pizza, thin crust, use the dough in the fridge, and taste it before plating," you get what you wanted.

The skill is not pressing buttons — it is writing the recipe clearly and tasting the dish before it goes out. Vague request in, vague code out.

Analogy 2: a brilliant intern with no memory

Think of the AI as a sharp intern who forgets everything overnight and has never seen your codebase before. They are fast and knowledgeable, but they do not know your conventions, your weird legacy file, or that one function you must never touch.

So you do two things every good manager does: you give them context (the relevant files, the rules, the goal) and you review their work before it ships. The AI's blind spots are exactly an intern's blind spots.

The loop that actually works

Strong workflows follow a tight loop. Beginners skip steps 1, 4, and 5 — that is where the pain comes from.

  1. Give context. Point the AI at the right files and tell it the rules. Many tools read a project file (Cursor's .cursorrules, or a CLAUDE.md for Claude Code) where you write standing instructions once.
  2. Ask for a plan first. For anything non-trivial, have the AI describe its approach before it writes code. Cheap to fix a wrong plan; expensive to fix wrong code.
  3. Let it generate. Small, focused changes beat one giant rewrite.
  4. Verify. Run the tests, run the app, read the diff (the list of exact lines changed). Never assume it works because it looks confident.
  5. Iterate. Feed errors and screenshots back in. "This test failed with X, fix it" is a powerful instruction.

A simple project instructions file removes most repeated friction:

# CLAUDE.md
- Use TypeScript, never plain JS.
- Tests live next to the file as *.test.ts; write one for every new function.
- Run `npm run lint` and `npm test` before saying a task is done.
- Never edit files in /legacy without asking.

Giving good context is really prompt engineering applied to code, and the standing-rules file is just a project-wide system prompt. Agents go further by using tool calling — the AI is allowed to run real commands like reading a file, running tests, or searching the repo, then reacts to what it sees.

A realistic agent session

# You type a goal; the agent does the loop itself.
$ claude "Add a /health endpoint that returns 200 and the app version. Add a test."

# Behind the scenes the agent will roughly:
#  1. grep the repo to find the router file
#  2. write the endpoint + a test
#  3. run `npm test`  ->  sees it pass
#  4. show you the diff for approval

The big risk is trust without checking. An AI will confidently produce code that imports a library that does not exist, deletes a line it should have kept, or "fixes" a test by weakening it. This is the coding version of a hallucination — fluent nonsense. Your verification step is the seatbelt.

⚠️ "Vibe coding" — accepting AI output without reading it — is fine for a throwaway prototype and dangerous for anything real. The faster the AI, the more important your review becomes, because mistakes ship faster too.

Done well, the payoff is large. Boilerplate, tests, refactors, and "explain this scary file" tasks go from hours to minutes — as long as you stay the editor-in-chief, not a passenger.
takeaways: Tools run from autocomplete (you drive) to agents (it loops alone).
Every task is one loop: context → plan → generate → verify → iterate.
Read the diff and run tests; never trust confident-looking code blindly.

diagram.caption: The same context-plan-generate-verify-iterate loop powers every AI coding tool, from autocomplete to autonomous agents.

quiz.q: You ask an agent to 'fix the bug' with no files, no error message, and no project rules. What is the most likely outcome?
options: It refuses to start until you confirm the bug
It guesses what you mean and often edits the wrong thing
It automatically reads your mind from git history
It produces perfect code because modern models rarely need context
quiz.explain: The AI only knows what you give it. With no context it has to guess, and guesses usually miss — which is why 'give context' is step one of the loop.

quiz.q: Which task is the best fit for an autonomous coding agent rather than inline autocomplete?
options: Finishing the variable name you are halfway through typing
Adding a feature across several files, then running and fixing the tests
Suggesting the rest of a single line in a for-loop
Auto-closing a bracket you just opened
quiz.explain: Agents shine on multi-step goals that need reading files, editing, and running tests in a loop. The other options are exactly what lightweight autocomplete is for.

quiz.q: An agent reports a previously failing test now passes. Before trusting it, what should you check first?
options: That the model used was the most expensive one
That the test still asserts the real behaviour, not a weakened version
That the diff is large enough to look like real work
quiz.explain: A lazy agent can make a test 'green' by deleting its assertion. Verifying the test still checks what you care about catches this; a bigger or more expensive run proves nothing.

quiz.q: Why is putting standing rules in a CLAUDE.md or .cursorrules file better than re-typing them in every prompt?
options: It makes the model run faster on the GPU
It permanently fine-tunes the model on your repo
It applies your conventions consistently without repetition or being lost from a long chat
It lets you skip reading the diff entirely
quiz.explain: The rules file acts as a project-wide system prompt: consistent context every time, and not at risk of falling out of a long chat's context window. It does not fine-tune the model or replace verification.

mentalModel: Treat the AI like a brilliant intern with amnesia: it is fast and knowledgeable but has never seen your codebase and forgets everything overnight. So every task is the same loop — give context, ask for a plan, let it work, then read the diff and run the tests before you trust a single line.
mistakes: Skipping context. Asking "fix the bug" with no files, no error message, and no rules. The AI guesses, and guesses are usually wrong. Always point it at the relevant code and paste the actual error.

Accepting code without reading it. Confident formatting is not correctness. AI happily invents functions and libraries that do not exist (a hallucination). Read the diff every time; run the tests every time.

Asking for one giant change. "Rebuild the whole auth system" produces a sprawling diff you cannot review. Break work into small, verifiable steps so each one can be checked and rolled back on its own.

Letting the context window overflow on a long session. When you dump a huge codebase into a single chat, important early instructions fall out of the context window and the AI 'forgets' them. Start fresh sessions for new tasks and keep standing rules in a project file instead.

Trusting the AI to fix its own failing test by changing the test. A lazy agent may make a test pass by deleting the assertion. Verify that the test still actually checks the behaviour you care about, not just that it is green.

Using a chat model for an agent job, or vice versa. Inline autocomplete is for finishing a line; an agent is for multi-step tasks. Reaching for the heavy agent to rename one variable wastes time and tokens; reaching for autocomplete to refactor ten files will not work.
exercise:
Pick a small real repo (or make a tiny one with a single function and no tests). Using an agent-style tool such as Claude Code or Cursor's agent mode, write a project rules file (CLAUDE.md or .cursorrules) with at least three rules — for example: use TypeScript, write a test for every function, and run the test suite before declaring done. Then give the agent one focused task: "Add input validation to function X and a test that proves it rejects bad input." Before accepting anything, read the full diff and run the tests yourself. Note one thing the AI got right and one thing you had to correct.

Stretch goal: Deliberately give the agent a vague version of the same task ("make function X better") and compare the result to the specific version. Write two or three sentences on how the quality and reviewability changed, and what that teaches you about giving context.
solution: Hint: The whole point is the loop, not the typing. A good run looks like: the agent reads your rules file, proposes a small change touching one function plus one test, you read the diff (watching for invented imports or a test that asserts nothing real), then you run the suite and see it pass. For the vague-versus-specific comparison, you will almost always find the vague prompt produces a bigger, less predictable diff that is harder to review — concrete proof that your job is supplying context and acceptance criteria, not just hitting accept. If the agent claims it is done but never ran the tests, that is your cue to tighten the rules file: add an explicit 'run `npm test` and paste the output before finishing' line.
summary: An AI coding workflow is a habit, not a button: give context, ask for a plan, generate in small steps, then verify by reading the diff and running tests. The tools range from autocomplete (you drive) to copilots (it suggests, you approve) to agents (it loops on its own). Treat the AI as a fast intern with no memory of your codebase — its power is real, but only if you stay the reviewer instead of blindly trusting confident-looking output.

keyTerms.def: The repeatable way a developer uses AI to write and change code: setting context, requesting work, verifying it, and iterating.
Inline AI suggestions that finish the line or block you are typing, like GitHub Copilot's grey ghost text. You stay in control of every change.
An in-editor AI that proposes edits you review and approve, rather than acting on its own. It suggests; you decide.
An AI that takes a goal and runs autonomously — reading files, writing code, running tests, and fixing errors in a loop until the task looks done.
The exact list of lines added and removed by a change. Reading the diff is how you verify what the AI actually did, line by line.
A file like CLAUDE.md or .cursorrules where you write standing instructions (conventions, do-nots, how to verify) so you do not repeat them in every prompt.
The files, rules, errors, and goal you give the AI so it can do the task. The AI knows nothing about your codebase except what you provide.
Accepting AI-written code without reading it. Acceptable for throwaway prototypes; risky for anything you actually ship and maintain.
Running tests, running the app, and reading the diff to confirm the AI's output is correct before trusting it. The safety belt of any AI workflow.
